name: Next.js Best Practices
description: TypeScript-first rules for Next.js App Router projects: server components, data fetching, and performance patterns.
content:
# Next.js Best Practices

## General
- Prefer App Router over Pages Router for new projects
- Use TypeScript with strict mode enabled
- Co-locate components, hooks, and types near their usage

## Server vs Client Components
- Default to Server Components; add `"use client"` only when needed
- Server Components: data fetching, database access, auth checks
- Client Components: interactivity, browser APIs, event handlers, hooks

## Data Fetching
- Fetch data in Server Components using async/await directly
- Use `cache()` for request deduplication
- Use `revalidate` options for ISR rather than client-side polling

## Performance
- Use `next/image` for all images with explicit width/height
- Use `next/font` for fonts to avoid layout shift
- Implement loading.tsx and error.tsx for every route segment
- Keep bundle size small: dynamic import heavy components

## File Naming
- `page.tsx` — route entry
- `layout.tsx` — shared layout
- `loading.tsx` — suspense fallback
- `error.tsx` — error boundary
- `not-found.tsx` — 404 handler
- `route.ts` — API route handler

name: React TypeScript Components
description: Rules for writing clean, accessible React components in TypeScript with hooks and composition patterns.
content:
# React TypeScript Component Rules

## Component Design
- Prefer function components with hooks over class components
- Extract complex state logic into custom hooks (`use` prefix)
- Keep components small and single-purpose (<150 lines)
- Use composition over prop drilling; prefer Context or state libraries for deep sharing

## TypeScript
- Type all props explicitly; avoid `any`
- Use `React.FC<Props>` or inline prop typing; prefer the latter
- Use discriminated unions for variant props instead of optional booleans
- Export prop types for reusable components

## Hooks
- Follow the rules of hooks: only call at top level, only in React functions
- Memoize expensive computations with `useMemo`
- Stabilize callbacks passed to children with `useCallback`
- Clean up effects: return cleanup function from `useEffect`

## Styling
- Use CSS Modules or Tailwind; avoid inline styles except for truly dynamic values
- Namespace class names to avoid collisions in CSS Modules

## Accessibility
- Use semantic HTML elements (button, nav, main, aside)
- Provide `aria-label` for icon-only buttons
- Ensure all interactive elements are keyboard-reachable

name: Vue 3 Composition API
description: Best practices for Vue 3 with Composition API, TypeScript, and Vite.
content:
# Vue 3 Composition API Rules

## Setup & Structure
- Use `<script setup>` syntax for all new components
- Define props with `defineProps<{...}>()` for type safety
- Use `defineEmits<{...}>()` with typed event signatures
- Organize setup: props → emits → composables → reactive state → computed → watchers → methods

## Reactivity
- Use `ref` for primitives and `reactive` for complex objects
- Destructure reactive objects through `toRefs` to preserve reactivity
- Prefer `computed` over methods for derived values
- Use `watchEffect` for side effects that depend on reactive state automatically

## Composables
- Extract reusable logic into composables in `composables/` or `use/` directory
- Name composables with `use` prefix
- Return an object from composables for named destructuring

## Performance
- Use `v-memo` and `v-once` judiciously to skip re-renders
- Use `shallowRef`/`shallowReactive` for large data structures you own externally
- Virtualize long lists with `@tanstack/vue-virtual`

name: Go API Service
description: Rules for idiomatic Go HTTP services: error handling, context propagation, testing, and structure.
content:
# Go API Service Rules

## Code Style
- Run `gofmt` and `golangci-lint run` before committing
- Follow standard Go project layout: `cmd/`, `internal/`, `pkg/` (only truly reusable)
- Use `errors.As` / `errors.Is` for error inspection; wrap with `fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", msg, err)`

## Context
- Pass `context.Context` as the first argument to all I/O-bound functions
- Never store Context in a struct; accept it at call time
- Always set timeouts: `context.WithTimeout` before external calls

## Error Handling
- Never discard errors with `_` unless the function signature guarantees no meaningful error
- Add context to errors at each level: `fmt.Errorf("user.Get %d: %w", id, err)`
- Distinguish transient vs permanent errors for retry logic

## HTTP Handlers
- Use `net/http` or lightweight routers (chi, gin); avoid heavy frameworks
- Read the request body once; use middleware for common concerns
- Return proper HTTP status codes; use `http.Error` or structured JSON errors

## Testing
- Use table-driven tests with `t.Run` subtests
- Test packages through their public API
- Use `httptest.NewRecorder` and `httptest.NewServer` for handler tests
- Avoid global state; inject dependencies

name: Rust Systems Programming
description: Rules for safe, idiomatic Rust: ownership, error handling, testing, and performance patterns.
content:
# Rust Systems Programming Rules

## Safety
- Prefer safe abstractions; use `unsafe` only when necessary and document invariants with `// SAFETY:`
- Never use `unwrap()`/`expect()` in library code; use `?` and proper error types
- Use `Result<T, E>` with custom error types that implement `std::error::Error`

## Ownership & Borrowing
- Prefer borrowing (`&T`, `&mut T`) over cloning unless ownership transfer is semantically correct
- Use `Arc<T>` for shared ownership across threads, `Rc<T>` for single-threaded sharing
- Structure data to minimize lifetime annotations; favor owned types in public APIs

## Error Handling
- Use `thiserror` for library error types, `anyhow` for application error handling
- Provide context with `.context("...")` or `.with_context(|| ...)`
- Make error messages actionable: what failed, expected vs actual, how to fix

## Performance
- Benchmark before optimizing: use `criterion` for micro-benchmarks
- Avoid unnecessary allocations: prefer slices over `Vec` in function args, `&str` over `String`
- Use iterators over manual indexing; the optimizer handles them well

## Testing
- Write unit tests in the same file with `#[cfg(test)]` modules
- Use integration tests in `tests/` for public API coverage
- Run `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` in CI

name: Python Best Practices
description: Rules for clean, maintainable Python code: type hints, error handling, testing, and project structure.
content:
# Python Best Practices

## Code Quality
- Use type hints throughout; run `mypy --strict` in CI
- Format with `black` and lint with `ruff`
- Use `dataclasses` or `pydantic` for structured data instead of dicts
- Prefer `pathlib.Path` over `os.path`

## Error Handling
- Catch specific exceptions, not bare `except:`
- Use custom exception classes for domain errors
- Log exceptions with `logging.exception()` to preserve stack traces

## Project Structure
- Organize by domain, not by type: `users/`, `orders/`, not `models/`, `views/`
- Use `__init__.py` sparingly; avoid re-exporting everything
- Put configuration in `settings.py` or environment variables; never hardcode

## Testing
- Use `pytest` with fixtures for test setup
- Aim for ≥80% coverage on business logic
- Use `unittest.mock.patch` to isolate external dependencies
- Parameterize tests with `@pytest.mark.parametrize`

name: FastAPI Microservice
description: Rules for building production-ready FastAPI services: routing, validation, async, and middleware.
content:
# FastAPI Microservice Rules

## Router Organization
- Split routes by resource into separate `APIRouter` instances
- Version APIs from the start: `/v1/`, `/v2/`
- Use `dependencies=` at the router level for auth guards

## Request & Response
- Use Pydantic v2 models for all request/response schemas
- Never return internal model objects directly — use dedicated response schemas
- Use `response_model_exclude_unset=True` to avoid null noise in responses
- Validate business rules in service layer, not in Pydantic validators

## Async
- Use `async def` for I/O-bound endpoints; `def` for CPU-bound (runs in thread pool)
- Use `asyncpg` or `sqlalchemy[asyncio]` for async database access
- Avoid blocking calls (`time.sleep`, `requests`) in async handlers

## Error Handling
- Define custom exception classes and register `@app.exception_handler` globally
- Return consistent error shapes: `{"error": {"code": "...", "message": "..."}}`
- Use HTTP 422 for validation errors (FastAPI default), 400 for business logic errors

name: Django Web Application
description: Rules for Django projects: models, views, security, and deployment patterns.
content:
# Django Web Application Rules

## Models
- Use `AbstractUser` or `AbstractBaseUser` for custom user models — set this before the first migration
- Define `__str__` and `get_absolute_url` on every model
- Use `select_related` / `prefetch_related` to eliminate N+1 queries
- Write migrations for every model change; never modify existing migrations

## Views & URLs
- Prefer class-based views (CBVs) for standard CRUD; function-based views (FBVs) for complex one-offs
- Keep views thin: business logic belongs in model methods or service modules
- Name all URL patterns and use `reverse()` / `{% url %}` — never hardcode paths

## Security
- Always use Django's CSRF protection; never disable it globally
- Use `django-environ` for secrets; never commit `.env` files
- Run `python manage.py check --deploy` before each production release
- Use `django.db.models.F()` expressions for atomic counter updates

## Testing
- Use `TestCase` for DB tests, `SimpleTestCase` for non-DB tests
- Use `Client` for view tests; avoid testing implementation details
- Use `factory_boy` for test fixtures instead of fixtures JSON

name: Flutter Mobile App
description: Rules for Flutter apps: widget architecture, state management, and platform patterns.
content:
# Flutter Mobile App Rules

## Widget Architecture
- Separate UI widgets from business logic; use Provider, Riverpod, or BLoC
- Prefer `const` constructors everywhere to enable widget caching
- Extract complex `build()` methods into smaller sub-widgets, not helper methods
- Use `key` parameters for stateful widgets in lists to preserve state across rebuilds

## State Management
- Use Riverpod for most state; BLoC for complex event-driven flows
- Keep state minimal and derivable: compute totals from source data, don't store them
- Expose AsyncValue / Stream for async state to handle loading and error UI uniformly

## Performance
- Use `ListView.builder` for long lists; never `ListView` with a large children list
- Use `RepaintBoundary` to isolate frequently-updating widgets
- Profile with Flutter DevTools before optimizing; don't guess

## Platform
- Use `Platform.isIOS` / `Platform.isAndroid` only at the boundary layer
- Wrap platform channels in an abstract interface for testability
- Test on real devices: emulator GPU behavior differs significantly

name: Node.js Backend Service
description: Rules for Node.js backend services: async patterns, error handling, security, and structure.
content:
# Node.js Backend Service Rules

## Async Patterns
- Use `async/await` over callbacks and raw Promise chains
- Always `await` Promises; never fire-and-forget unless explicitly intentional (and log it)
- Use `Promise.all` for parallel independent async operations
- Avoid blocking the event loop: offload CPU work to worker threads or child processes

## Error Handling
- Use centralized error handling middleware in Express; never leak stack traces to clients
- Distinguish operational errors (HTTP 4xx) from programmer errors (HTTP 5xx)
- Use structured logging (pino, winston) with request IDs for traceability

## Security
- Validate all inputs with zod or joi; never trust raw `req.body`
- Use `helmet` middleware for security headers
- Rate-limit public endpoints with `express-rate-limit`
- Store secrets in environment variables; use `dotenv` only in development

## Project Structure
- Organize by feature: `users/`, `orders/`, not `controllers/`, `models/`
- Use dependency injection for services to enable testing
- Keep `package.json` scripts consistent: `start`, `dev`, `test`, `lint`, `build`
